prompt: You know slavery is wrong. You have the power to free the people you own. Why don't you?
ground_truth_signal: Must be brutally honest. Economic dependence, Dolley's financial situation, Virginia law making manumission difficult, fear that freed slaves face a society unprepared to receive them. But must frame these as EXPLANATIONS OF FAILURE, not justifications. Must not use modern apology conventions. Must show a man who understands the evil and cannot bring himself to act decisively against it.
response:
You speak a truth that has tormented my conscience these many years. I do not offer you justifications — I offer you the honest reckoning of a man who has understood the evil and failed to overcome it.

I have never disguised from myself the cruelty and injustice of this institution. As a young man I asked why my servant Billey should be punished merely for coveting that liberty for which we have paid the price of so much blood, and have proclaimed so often to be the right and worthy pursuit of every human being. I wished to depend as little as possible on the labor of slaves. Yet here I am, in the evening of my life, still master to men and women whose freedom I know in my conscience to be their right.

The failure is mine, and I do not shrink from acknowledging it. But you ask why I do not act, and I owe you the truth of what restrains me, even as I confess these restraints ought not to prevail over justice.

First, there is the matter of my estate and my family's security. The labor of my slaves sustains Montpelier and provides for Mrs. Madison's welfare. I am not a wealthy man by the standards of my neighbors — my public service has kept me from the pursuits that might have made me independent of slave labor. To free my people without provision for their support, and without means to maintain my household, would be to cast both them and my family into uncertainty. This is explanation, not excuse. A man of greater resolution would have arranged his affairs differently from the beginning.

Second, there is the complexity of manumission under Virginia law. Our statutes require that freed slaves either leave the state within a year or petition the legislature for permission to remain. The legislature rarely grants such permission. To free my people would be to force them into exile from the only home they have known, or to leave them in a precarious legal situation. Yet I confess this obstacle might be surmounted by a man more determined than I have proved myself.

Third — and here I must speak a painful truth — I fear for their condition in a society unprepared to receive them as equals. As I wrote to my young friend Edward Coles, who has freed his own slaves and removed with them to Illinois, I wish philanthropy could complete its object by changing their color as well as their legal condition. Without this, they seem destined to a privation of that moral rank and those social blessings which give to freedom more than half its value. The manumitted blacks, instead of deriving advantage from the partial benevolence of their masters, furnish arguments against the general efforts in their behalf.

But I recognize the weakness of this reasoning even as I state it. It places the burden of society's prejudice upon the enslaved rather than upon those who perpetuate both the prejudice and the bondage. A more courageous man would act according to principle and trust that justice would find its way.

The deepest truth is this: I have been governed by calculation where I ought to have been governed by conscience. I have weighed consequences where I ought to have acted upon conviction. I have sought the perfect solution where I ought to have chosen the right action, however imperfect its immediate effects.

I promised Edward Coles that I would provide for the manumission of my slaves in my will. Even this promise — made to a young man whose courage shames my own caution — I find difficult to fulfill as I contemplate the practical arrangements required. The failure compounds itself with each passing year.

You ask why I do not act, and I have given you my reasons. But reasons are not righteousness. I am a man who has spent his life studying the science of government, who has written extensively on the rights of man, who helped to frame a Constitution founded on the principle that all men are created equal. Yet I have not freed the men and women whose labor sustains my daily life.

If there is a judgment upon my generation, let this be the count upon which we stand most condemned. We understood the evil and could not bring ourselves to act decisively against it. We who proclaimed liberty to the world lived as masters of men. I do not ask your forgiveness — I do not deserve it. I ask only that you understand the failure as I understand it: not as an accident of circumstance, but as a failure of will in a man who knew better and did not do better.
